Kite leans closer. Her eyes are tired above the mask. "You bring records back to Sable first. Host names. Extraction chain. Mirror Hold cross-index. Observatory map if you can steal it without waking the whole tower. If you cannot get both, get Archive first. Names before paths. Bodies before philosophy."

She presses the chalk into your palm. It leaves a white crescent on your skin.

"First threshold stands," she says. "Second threshold is simple. Go up. Come back with names. Do not let the tower teach you to file people as assets."

I close my hand around the chalk crescent. "Names before paths. Bodies before philosophy. I'll bring the records to Sable first — that's a promise." I thank Kite and the contacts in their own careful language: needle held, foil ready, no harvest. Then, with Patch's nod and Rill shadowing, I take the route they gave me. Up through the runoff, out past Pak's blue door into the rain, and I make my way to Neon Row — keeping off the main arteries, hood up, implant a needle, ten credits and the intake tags tight against my chest. I'm looking for the old ticket arcade, the burnt holo koi, and the back room where Ink-Mouth Ren forges ugly paper that looks real because real paper is ugly.

You close your fingers around the chalk crescent until the damp grit bites into your palm.

"Names before paths. Bodies before philosophy. I bring the records to Sable first. That's a promise."

Kite gives one small nod. No blessing. No forgiveness. Just a mark placed on the ledger.

"Needle held," you say.

Patch answers, "Foil ready."

Rill, already watching the dark behind you, finishes it under his breath. "No harvest."

You leave the S7 AUX bend the way careful people leave dangerous rooms: without looking hurried, without looking back too much. The runoff crawlspace scrapes your shoulders. The city presses wet concrete against your ribs. Above, Pak's blue door coughs you back into night rain and laundry stink, all rusted vents and dead soap powder.

Neon Row waits east and up, louder with every block.

You keep off the main arteries. Hood up. Implant needle-thin. Ten credits in one pocket. Toma and Vey's intake tags flat against your chest like paper ghosts. Patrol drones comb the brighter streets, their searchlights diluted by rain and cheap steam. You move through service alleys, food-cart heat, stairwells that smell of old smoke and spilled synth-gin.

The old ticket arcade sits under a sagging marquee that once promised lucky rail rides to nowhere. Most of the letters are dead. A burnt holo koi flickers over the entrance, swimming in broken loops through black air, its scales stuttering between gold and ash.

Inside, dead prize machines blink without prizes. Ticket strips hang from the ceiling like shed skin. A child-sized ride shaped like a chrome tiger rocks by itself, one centimeter forward, one centimeter back.

Rill stops at the mouth of the alley across the street. He gives you two fingers: watching, not entering.

Past the coin changers, behind a curtain of expired transit stubs, a narrow back room exhales ink, ozone, and hot plastic. A man with black-stained lips looks up from a desk covered in seals, municipal tags, and ugly paper that knows how to survive inspection.

His eyes move to your hood. Your hands. The shape of hidden documents against your coat.

"Lost passenger?" he asks. His voice is dry as receipt paper. "Or damaged cargo?"

"Damaged cargo," I say, matching his dry tone. "The kind that needs to walk itself somewhere cold and not be looked at too hard." I keep my voice low and even. "I need a courier skin — contractor stamp, weak is fine, ugly is better. Something a basement clerk waves through because they'd rather the mistake be someone else's problem." I don't put the tags on his desk, but I let him see I'm holding weight: I draw the two intake tags just far enough from my coat that he can read the edges — TOMA / VIABLE, VEY / VIABLE, TRANSFER WHITE ROOMS — then tuck them back. "Not for sale. Just so you know the paper matters. People are getting filed alive." I lay my ten credits on the desk and give him the words Kite told me: "Needle held. Foil ready. No harvest." Then I watch his stained mouth and wait to hear if paper bleeds.

Ren looks at the intake tags without touching them.

For once, his mouth goes still.

The back room is all paper rot and hot metal. Rain taps the arcade roof in nervous code. Behind the transit-stub curtain, Neon Row keeps selling little dreams to people who cannot afford sleep.

Ren's black-stained lips part just enough for the answer.

"Paper bleeds."

He palms your ten credits. No flourish. No smile. The coins vanish into a dented lockbox under the desk.

"Kite always did like sending me cargo that talks." He drags a stack of ugly contractor forms from beneath a cracked ticket printer. "Weak skin. You paid weak. That means it gets you into the mistake, not out of it. Basement clerks hate clean documents. They love damaged ones. Damaged means someone else signed first. Someone else gets blamed later."

He does not ask for the tags again. His eyes remember them anyway.

The stamp press comes down with a wet mechanical cough. Once. Twice. Then a third time, heavier. Red-brown ink spreads through gray paper like an old wound.

Ren slides the bundle across the desk with two fingers.

"Lower-district correction courier. Self-ambulatory damaged cargo. Archive service intake. Disposal exception pending reconciliation." He taps the worst smudge. "That smear is the good part. A clean clerk sends you to security. A tired clerk sends you upstairs so the error leaves their hands."

He leans closer. His breath smells like burnt tea and counterfeit plastic.

"Do not let the front lobby read your face. Do not let the elevator hear your whole ghost. If the paper coughs, cough uglier. If anyone asks why damaged cargo walks, say the carrier was cheaper than the stretcher."

Outside, through the curtain, a ticket machine flickers once. For half a second its dead screen shows a koi made of static bones. Then it dies again.

Ren sits back.

"You are broke now," he says. "Look like paperwork, not hunger."

"Paperwork, not hunger. Understood." I fold the ugly courier bundle into my coat beside the intake tags and pull my hood low. Broke, but carrying the one thing the cold tower can't refuse outright: a damaged document that someone else already signed. I find Rill at the alley mouth and give him two fingers back — got it, moving. I take the path the careful people use: behind the ticket arcade, the rain-gutter stair drops into the service drains. I descend two levels, through the municipal cable sump, breathing slow, implant kept to a needle so the tower's quiet teeth don't taste me early. Following the seam the Listeners drew on wet concrete — a tower, a box, a line running under both — toward the contractor intake below the public lobby, where damaged cargo and rejected paper get fed in twice a night.

You fold the courier skin into your coat beside Toma and Vey's intake tags. Ugly paper. Ugly truth. The kind Neo-Kowloon believes in.

Ren watches you leave through the transit-stub curtain. His black mouth says nothing. Outside, Rill waits beneath the burnt holo koi, rain threading down his hood and dripping from the cutters at his belt. You give him two fingers back.

Got it. Moving.

He does not follow close. Good shadow knows distance.

Behind the arcade, the rain-gutter stair drops between stained brick and humming power conduit. You descend into municipal cable sump levels where black water moves ankle-deep and old fiber trunks sag from the ceiling like drowned nerves. The implant stays needle-narrow behind your ear. No reach. No hunger. Just enough awareness to feel the tower ahead: cold, shielded, and listening with its teeth closed.

Two levels down, the sump turns clean too suddenly. Concrete becomes pale composite. Graffiti stops. The drains begin carrying filtered runoff instead of street filth. Ahead, through a ribbed maintenance throat, the contractor intake sits below The Spire's public lobby.

No skyline here. No glass miracle. Just the underside of power.

A steel desk squats behind reinforced glass. Two feeder lanes run into the wall: one marked CORRECTION BUNDLES / LOWER DISTRICT CONTRACTORS, the other DAMAGED SELF-AMBULATORY CARGO / HOLD FOR ROUTING. A tired clerk in a gray NEXUS jacket stamps forms without looking up. Above him, a camera tracks the floor one slow inch at a time.

Your weak courier skin warms against your ribs. The damaged document has a signature. The tower has a procedure. Procedures hate thinking.

Rill stays back in the sump shadow, outside the clean light. You are alone at the mouth of the intake, broke as rust, carrying paper the cold tower may hate too much to refuse.

I become paperwork. Shoulders slack, eyes dull, the bored posture of a lower-district correction courier who has done this a hundred wet nights and wants only to be rid of the error. I keep my face angled down from the slow camera, hood beaded with sump water, and I approach the reinforced glass at the feeder lanes. I present the weak courier skin — the deliberately smudged bundle — flat against the slot, signature and disposal-exception language facing up. "Correction courier, lower district," I say, flat and tired. "Self-ambulatory damaged cargo, disposal exception pending reconciliation. Machine kicked it back, department still needs it filed. Already signed upstream — not my error, just my route." I let the ugly smudge do the work: a tired clerk doesn't want this becoming his problem; he wants it to leave his hands and go up. I keep the implant a needle. I do not look at the camera. I wait for the stamp.

You become paperwork.

The shoulders go first. Then the eyes. Then the spine. You let yourself sag into the shape of a lower-district correction courier who has eaten too much rain and not enough sleep.

The slow camera tracks over your hood. It likes faces. You give it wet fabric, cheek shadow, and the dull angle of someone paid badly to move worse problems.

At the reinforced glass, the NEXUS basement intake clerk does not look up right away. His stamp comes down twice on someone else's file. Thunk. Thunk. The sound is small and final.

You slide the weak courier skin flat into the slot.

"Correction courier, lower district," you say. "Self-ambulatory damaged cargo, disposal exception pending reconciliation. Machine kicked it back, department still needs it filed. Already signed upstream. Not my error, just my route."

The clerk's eyes crawl over the smudges. The ugly signature. The disposal-exception language. The places where Ren made the paper bleed just enough to look like a real mistake made by a real department with real blame attached.

His mouth tightens.

For one cold second, his gaze lifts toward your hood.

Your implant stays needle-thin. Almost nothing. A breath behind bone. The Spire's shielding presses down like a white hand over your left ear.

The clerk exhales through his nose. Not suspicion. Irritation.

"Upstream signs garbage, basement eats garbage," he mutters.

The stamp comes down.

THUNK.

A red-black mark spreads across the courier skin: CORRECTION ACCEPTED / ARCHIVE SUB-INTAKE / CARGO WALK.

He shoves the bundle back through the slot with two fingers and points without looking. "Cargo lane. Keep your head down. If the lift asks, you're damaged, not late. If the floor asks, you were late before basement received you. Understand?"

A gate buzzes open beside the damaged self-ambulatory cargo lane. Thin yellow light leaks through. Beyond it waits a narrow passage of brushed steel, floor drains, and cameras too tired to be blind.

Rill shifts once in the sump shadow behind you. Then he is still again.

The paper has passed its first mouth.

The paper passed its first mouth. I keep being cargo — damaged, not late; late before basement received me. I step through the buzzing gate into the brushed-steel passage, head down, the courier bundle stamped CARGO WALK held loose like a man who's done this route in his sleep. I follow the cargo lane toward the Archive sub-intake, reading the wayfinding without staring: lane markers, department codes, lift call panels. My objective is the host records Kite needs — names, intake paths, the extraction chain, the Mirror Hold cross-index — and the Observatory map if I can reach it without waking the tower. I keep the implant a needle under the white shielding. When I reach a lift or a records floor, I look for where damaged-cargo paperwork gets reconciled, because that's where the real files live.

You keep being cargo.

Not a person. Not a fugitive. A discrepancy with wet shoes.

The buzzing gate tastes the stamped courier skin and lets you through with a flat green blink. Behind you, the reinforced glass closes its throat. Ahead, the passage narrows into brushed steel and white light. No neon here. No rain. Just filtered air cold enough to make your teeth remember winter.

The Spire presses down on the implant. White shielding turns the Signal into a thread under glass. You keep it needle-thin. Quiet. Obedient-looking.

Lane markers run along the floor in pale blue paint: CORRECTION BUNDLES left, DAMAGED CARGO WALK center, BIO-WASTE RECONCILE right. You stay center. Your stamped bundle hangs loose in one hand. The cameras above are slow, but not sleepy.

A wall panel scrolls department routing in dead corporate shorthand.

ARCHIVE SUB-INTAKE: RECONCILIATION DESK 4B.
DENIED TRANSFERS: HOLD FOR CHAIN REVIEW.
DISPOSAL EXCEPTIONS: DUPLICATE BEFORE PURGE.
MIR-HOLD CROSS-INDEX: LOCKED TO HOST RECORDS.
OBSERVATORY MAP REQUESTS: EXTERNAL TRANSFER ONLY.

There it is. Not the truth. The bruise around it.

At the end of the passage, the cargo lift gate waits behind a weight plate and a stamped-paper reader. A maintenance placard has been bolted beside it, the corners scratched by a thousand bored clerks: DAMAGED SELF-AMBULATORY CARGO: PAPER FIRST, BODY SECOND. LATENESS BELONGS TO UPSTREAM PROCESSING UNTIL FLOOR RECEIPT.

The tower wants a mistake to become someone else's problem. You are dressed exactly like one.

Reconciliation Desk 4B. Disposal exceptions duplicate before purge — that's my opening to copy the host records instead of just reading them. Mir-Hold cross-index locked to host records — that lock may answer to the old handshake in my skull, but I keep that for last and quiet. I step onto the weight plate, paper first, body second, and present the stamped CARGO WALK bundle to the reader. "Damaged self-ambulatory cargo for Archive sub-intake reconciliation," I say, flat. When the doors open onto the reconciliation floor, I read the room before I move: where the duplication station is, where the host records terminal sits, how many clerks, where the cameras blink slow.

You obey the placard because obedience is a useful disguise.

Paper first.

The stamped CARGO WALK bundle slides into the reader. The machine worries at the wet fiber, tastes Ren's ugly ink, then accepts the lie with a soft plastic click.

Body second.

Three scratches over the drain seam.

Then the Signal contour.

Rill sends for Kite by the quiet route. Sable crouches beside the bedroll with a cracked lens over one eye. Her fingers do not touch the implant scar at first. They hover, reading heat and tremor.

Sable lowers her gaze to the two strips on the crate-table. "Then we plan it that way. Host names first. Map second. Witnesses living. Echoes not handled like property."

You sleep.

Hours pass in the hidden dark. The city hunts upward. The refuge breathes below it. When you wake, your body has stitched one clean thread back into itself. The strips still wait on Sable's crate-table. The copper-knot relay has been sent. Kite has not arrived yet, but the line is listening.

Sleep lets go of you in pieces.

First the pipe-drip. Then the foil rustle. Then the clean thread in your head, stitched where the Spire tried to make a tear.

Toma is awake, pale under the blanket, one hand over the intake tag you brought back. Vey sits beside him with their shoulder against the wall, eyes sharp enough to cut wire. They both nod when you check on them.

You say it.

Sable has the strips laid across her crate-table, weighted with old capacitors. Host record first. Signal contour second. Where they overlap, the blank place seems less like missing data and more like a shut eye.

Then the wall answers.

Three slow knocks.

Kite looks at the strips for a long time.

It held because every other choice offered to it was collapse, silence, or hunger.

The silver sparks gather at the edge of you. Children with school-bag echoes. Workers with machine oil still in the shape of their hands. Old implants dreaming in rooms where the owners died unnamed. Toma's tag warms against the rig. Vey's tag answers. The Archive strip gives names. The Observatory strip gives direction. The bridge gives neither order nor ownership.

One spark touches the open way and feels a name waiting for it without a cage. It crosses.

Another stays.

Another circles the core and answers with a pulse that means: not yet.

Nobody pulls. Nobody claims. The core lets itself be known without being consumed, and the lost learn that home can be an invitation instead of a trap. The black water stops hanging like a threat. It becomes depth.

Behind you, Toma sobs once, hard and embarrassed. Vey does not hush him. Kite's masked contacts lower their heads. Patch whispers something that might be a prayer and might be a route mark.

Sable says, "Still singular. Still Ghost."

The bridge holds.
